sub_modules.title: Shape of the Earth
sub_modules.content: Earth is an oblate spheroid (flattened at poles). Compression is ~0.3%. Slight pear shape. True representation is called a geoid.

sub_modules.title: Geoid Models
sub_modules.content: Different models include WGS84, OS36, ED50. ICAO standard is WGS84. Differences can cause position errors.

sub_modules.title: Poles and Axis
sub_modules.content: Earth rotates about an axis tilted at 23.5°. Poles are endpoints of this axis.

sub_modules.title: Basic Directions
sub_modules.content: Cardinal directions: N, S, E, W. Quadrantal: NE, SE, SW, NW.

sub_modules.title: Sexagesimal System
sub_modules.content: Circle divided into 360°. Directions measured clockwise from North (True North).

sub_modules.title: True Direction
sub_modules.content: Measured from geographic North. Expressed as 000°–360° using 3 digits.

sub_modules.title: Reciprocal Direction
sub_modules.content: Opposite direction = ±180°.

sub_modules.title: Position Reference System
sub_modules.content: Uses Latitude and Longitude instead of Cartesian coordinates.

sub_modules.title: Great Circles
sub_modules.content: Largest circles on Earth. Shortest path between two points.

sub_modules.title: Equator
sub_modules.content: Great circle dividing Earth into two hemispheres. Reference for latitude.

sub_modules.title: Meridians
sub_modules.content: Lines joining poles. Reference for longitude.

sub_modules.title: Prime Meridian
sub_modules.content: 0° longitude passing through Greenwich.

sub_modules.title: Small Circles
sub_modules.content: Circles not centered at Earth's center (e.g., parallels of latitude).

sub_modules.title: Parallels of Latitude
sub_modules.content: East-West lines showing position North/South of Equator.

sub_modules.title: Graticule
sub_modules.content: Grid formed by latitude and longitude lines.

sub_modules.title: Angular Measurement
sub_modules.content: 1° = 60 minutes, 1 minute = 60 seconds.

sub_modules.title: Latitude
sub_modules.content: Angular distance from Equator (0°–90° N/S).

sub_modules.title: Geocentric vs Geodetic Latitude
sub_modules.content: Geodetic latitude is used in navigation charts.

sub_modules.title: Special Parallels
sub_modules.content: Tropics (23.5°), Arctic/Antarctic circles (66.5°).

sub_modules.title: Longitude
sub_modules.content: Angular distance from Prime Meridian (0°–180° E/W).

sub_modules.title: Difference in Longitude
sub_modules.content: Same name → subtract, different → add, choose shortest value.

sub_modules.title: Anti-Meridian
sub_modules.content: 180° longitude opposite Greenwich.

sub_modules.title: Latitude vs Longitude Difference
sub_modules.content: Latitudes are parallel; longitudes converge at poles.

sub_modules.title: Position Format
sub_modules.content: Latitude written first, then longitude (e.g., 51°N 001°W).

sub_modules.title: Distance Conversion
sub_modules.content: 1 minute of latitude = 1 nautical mile.

sub_modules.title: Accuracy and Resolution
sub_modules.content: Decimal minutes and seconds increase positional accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Great Circle Vertices
sub_modules.content: Highest and lowest latitude points on a great circle.

chapter_name: Chapter 2 - Great Circles, Rhumb Lines & Directions on the Earth

sub_modules.title: Great Circle Reminder
sub_modules.content: A Great Circle is the shortest path between two points on Earth.

sub_modules.title: Rhumb Line
sub_modules.content: A line of constant direction cutting all meridians at the same angle.

sub_modules.title: Great Circle vs Rhumb Line
sub_modules.content: Great Circle is shortest but changes direction; Rhumb Line is longer but constant direction.

sub_modules.title: Examples of Rhumb Lines
sub_modules.content: Parallels of latitude, meridians, and Equator.

sub_modules.title: Lines that are Both
sub_modules.content: Only Equator and meridians are both Great Circles and Rhumb Lines.

sub_modules.title: Great Circle Direction Change
sub_modules.content: Direction continuously changes and always bends toward the pole.

sub_modules.title: DIID Rule
sub_modules.content: Direction change: Decreasing-Increasing-Increasing-Decreasing.

sub_modules.title: Distance Units
sub_modules.content: Includes metre, kilometre, statute mile, nautical mile.

sub_modules.title: Nautical Mile
sub_modules.content: 1 NM = 1852 m = 1 minute of latitude.

sub_modules.title: Latitude Distance
sub_modules.content: 1° latitude = 60 NM, Equator to Pole = 5400 NM.

sub_modules.title: Longitude Distance
sub_modules.content: 1 minute longitude = 1 NM only at Equator.

sub_modules.title: Variation of NM Length
sub_modules.content: NM varies slightly due to Earth's shape.

sub_modules.title: Great Circle Distance Cases
sub_modules.content: Special cases include same meridian, opposite hemispheres, Equator routes.

sub_modules.title: Antipodal Points
sub_modules.content: Opposite points on Earth, distance = 10800 NM.

sub_modules.title: Mean Latitude
sub_modules.content: Average latitude between two points.

sub_modules.title: True Direction
sub_modules.content: Direction measured from geographic North (True North). Used on maps.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Direction
sub_modules.content: Direction measured from Magnetic North using a compass.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic North
sub_modules.content: Direction indicated by a freely suspended magnet.

sub_modules.title: Variation
sub_modules.content: Angle between True North and Magnetic North.

sub_modules.title: Isogonals
sub_modules.content: Lines joining points of equal variation.

sub_modules.title: Agonic Line
sub_modules.content: Line of zero variation.

sub_modules.title: Variation Changes
sub_modules.content: Changes over time due to Earth's magnetic field movement.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Dip
sub_modules.content: Angle between Earth's magnetic field and horizontal.

sub_modules.title: Horizontal and Vertical Components
sub_modules.content: Magnetic field splits into horizontal (H) and vertical (Z) components.

sub_modules.title: Dip Effects
sub_modules.content: Causes compass errors and reduces accuracy near poles.

sub_modules.title: Deviation
sub_modules.content: Error caused by aircraft magnetism affecting compass.

sub_modules.title: Variation Rule
sub_modules.content: West is best, East is least.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Rule
sub_modules.content: Deviation East Compass Least, Deviation West Compass Best.

sub_modules.title: Heading Types
sub_modules.content: True Heading, Magnetic Heading, Compass Heading.

sub_modules.title: Definitions
sub_modules.content: Includes variation, deviation, isogonals, dip, and related terms.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: The navigation computer is an analogue device used for solving navigation and flight planning problems quickly.

sub_modules.title: Importance in ATPL
sub_modules.content: Used in about 25% of exam questions and useful for faster calculations.

sub_modules.title: Navigation Computer Faces
sub_modules.content: Consists of a circular slide rule face and a wind face.

sub_modules.title: Slide Rule Theory
sub_modules.content: Based on logarithmic scales where multiplication becomes addition and division becomes subtraction.

sub_modules.title: Linear Scale Concept
sub_modules.content: Addition and subtraction using linear alignment of rulers.

sub_modules.title: Logarithmic Scale Concept
sub_modules.content: Multiplication and division using logarithmic scaling.

sub_modules.title: Circular Slide Rule
sub_modules.content: Conversion of linear slide rule into circular form for compact use.

sub_modules.title: Multiplication Method
sub_modules.content: Align 1 (10) on inner scale with a number on outer scale and read result.

sub_modules.title: Division Method
sub_modules.content: Align divisor on inner scale with dividend on outer scale and read result.

sub_modules.title: Decimal Placement
sub_modules.content: User must estimate magnitude mentally as scale shows only 10–99.9.

sub_modules.title: Approximation Technique
sub_modules.content: Rough mental calculation is essential before using the computer.

sub_modules.title: Rates and Ratios
sub_modules.content: Used for solving time, speed, distance, and fuel problems.

sub_modules.title: Proportion Method
sub_modules.content: Set up ratios directly on the computer for faster solutions.

chapter_name: Chapter 5 - Navigation Computer (Distance, Speed, Time and Conversions)

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: Uses ratio method on navigation computer for solving rate-based problems.

sub_modules.title: Base 1 and Base 60
sub_modules.content: Red arrow represents 1; black triangle represents 60 for hourly conversions.

sub_modules.title: Distance Speed Time Concept
sub_modules.content: Outer scale = distance/speed, inner scale = time.

sub_modules.title: Finding Distance
sub_modules.content: Set speed with 60 index, read distance against time.

sub_modules.title: Finding Speed
sub_modules.content: Set distance against time, read speed at 60 index.

sub_modules.title: Finding Time
sub_modules.content: Set speed with 60 index, read time against distance.

sub_modules.title: Yellow Scale
sub_modules.content: Used to convert minutes into hours and minutes.

sub_modules.title: Blue Scale (Ram Rise)
sub_modules.content: Used to calculate temperature rise due to air compression.

sub_modules.title: Fuel Consumption
sub_modules.content: Solved using same ratio method as distance-speed-time.

sub_modules.title: Distance Conversion
sub_modules.content: Convert between km, nautical miles, and statute miles.

sub_modules.title: Length Conversion
sub_modules.content: Convert metres to yards and feet.

sub_modules.title: Volume Conversion
sub_modules.content: Convert litres, imperial gallons, and US gallons.

sub_modules.title: Volume to Weight Conversion
sub_modules.content: Uses specific gravity to convert fuel volume to weight.

sub_modules.title: Specific Gravity Concept
sub_modules.content: Density ratio compared to water, used in fuel calculations.

chapter_name: Chapter 6 - Navigation Computer (TAS and Altitude Conversions)

sub_modules.title: Airspeed Types
sub_modules.content: IAS, CAS, EAS, TAS and their corrections.

sub_modules.title: IAS to CAS
sub_modules.content: Correct IAS for instrument and position errors.

sub_modules.title: CAS to TAS
sub_modules.content: Apply density correction to obtain True Airspeed.

sub_modules.title: Compressibility Correction
sub_modules.content: Applied when TAS exceeds 300 knots.

sub_modules.title: TAS from Mach Number
sub_modules.content: Calculate TAS using Mach and temperature.

sub_modules.title: True Altitude
sub_modules.content: Correct indicated altitude using temperature deviation.

sub_modules.title: Density Altitude
sub_modules.content: Altitude equivalent based on air density for performance.

sub_modules.title: Airspeed Correction Sequence
sub_modules.content: IAS → CAS → TAS → (Compressibility if needed).

sub_modules.title: Navigation Computer Windows
sub_modules.content: AIRSPEED, COMP CORR, ALTITUDE, DENSITY ALTITUDE.

sub_modules.title: Wind Face Introduction
sub_modules.content: Used to solve wind problems using triangle of velocities.

sub_modules.title: Heading
sub_modules.content: Direction aircraft nose is pointing.

sub_modules.title: Track
sub_modules.content: Actual path over ground.

sub_modules.title: Drift
sub_modules.content: Difference between heading and track.

sub_modules.title: Wind Correction Angle (WCA)
sub_modules.content: Angle applied to heading to maintain track.

sub_modules.title: Wind Effect
sub_modules.content: Wind causes aircraft to drift off intended path.

sub_modules.title: Air Vector
sub_modules.content: Heading and TAS (one arrow).

sub_modules.title: Wind Vector
sub_modules.content: Wind direction and speed (three arrows).

sub_modules.title: Ground Vector
sub_modules.content: Track and ground speed (two arrows).

sub_modules.title: Triangle of Velocities
sub_modules.content: Vector triangle combining air, wind, and ground vectors.

sub_modules.title: Wind Face Solution
sub_modules.content: Navigation computer solves triangle without drawing.

chapter_name: Chapter 8 - Navigation Computer (Heading and Wind Finding)

sub_modules.title: Wind Face Uses
sub_modules.content: Used to calculate track, heading, ground speed, and wind.

sub_modules.title: Find Track and Ground Speed
sub_modules.content: Given heading, TAS, and wind.

sub_modules.title: Find Heading and Ground Speed
sub_modules.content: Given track, TAS, and wind.

sub_modules.title: Wind Finding
sub_modules.content: Given heading, track, TAS, and ground speed.

sub_modules.title: Dead Reckoning (DR)
sub_modules.content: Estimating position using heading, speed, and time.

sub_modules.title: Mandatory Heading Use Case
sub_modules.content: Used when ATC assigns heading temporarily.

sub_modules.title: Heading Calculation
sub_modules.content: Adjust heading to counter drift and maintain track.

sub_modules.title: Drift Adjustment
sub_modules.content: Iterative correction of heading to match desired track.

sub_modules.title: Balancing the Drift
sub_modules.content: Refining heading until drift equals required correction.

chapter_name: Chapter 9 - Multi-drift Winds and Wind Components

sub_modules.title: Multi-drift Wind Method
sub_modules.content: Determining wind using drift observations on multiple headings.

sub_modules.title: Three-drift Wind Technique
sub_modules.content: Using three headings (ideally 60° apart) for accurate wind calculation.

sub_modules.title: Two-drift Wind Technique
sub_modules.content: Using two headings (at least 70° apart) for approximate wind calculation.

sub_modules.title: Cocked Hat Concept
sub_modules.content: Triangle formed when drift lines do not intersect perfectly, indicating error.

sub_modules.title: Wind Components
sub_modules.content: Resolving wind into head/tailwind and crosswind components.

sub_modules.title: Runway Wind Analysis
sub_modules.content: Using wind components for safe takeoff and landing decisions.

sub_modules.title: Crosswind Limits
sub_modules.content: Maximum allowable crosswind based on aircraft limitations.

sub_modules.title: Headwind Requirements
sub_modules.content: Minimum headwind required for safe landing performance.

sub_modules.title: Tailwind Calculations
sub_modules.content: Determining allowable tailwind limits and effects.

sub_modules.title: Maximum Allowable Wind Strength
sub_modules.content: Finding max wind speed for a given crosswind limit.

sub_modules.title: Allowable Wind Direction Range
sub_modules.content: Determining wind direction limits for safe operations.

sub_modules.title: True vs Magnetic Wind
sub_modules.content: Understanding difference between forecast and ATC wind directions.

sub_modules.title: 1 in 60 Rule Concept
sub_modules.content: 1 NM deviation after 60 NM equals 1° track error.

sub_modules.title: Basic Geometry
sub_modules.content: Angle approximately equals opposite/adjacent when adjacent is 60.

sub_modules.title: Track Error Calculation
sub_modules.content: Track error equals (distance off track / distance flown) × 60.

sub_modules.title: Track Made Good (TMG)
sub_modules.content: Actual track flown after deviation from planned track.

sub_modules.title: Scaling the Rule
sub_modules.content: Works for any distance using proportional scaling.

sub_modules.title: Approximation Accuracy
sub_modules.content: Based on 1 in 57.3 but approximated to 60 for simplicity.

sub_modules.title: Limitations
sub_modules.content: Accurate up to about 20°, best below 10°.

chapter_name: Chapter 11 - Navigation Using the 1 in 60 Rule

sub_modules.title: Track Error vs Drift
sub_modules.content: Track error is the difference between planned track and track made good, not drift.

sub_modules.title: Double Track Error Method
sub_modules.content: Turn double the track error angle to return to track.

sub_modules.title: Track Error and Closing Angle
sub_modules.content: Turn equals track error angle plus closing angle.

sub_modules.title: Closing Angle Calculation
sub_modules.content: Closing angle = (distance off / distance remaining) × 60.

sub_modules.title: Single Calculation Method
sub_modules.content: Turn = closing angle × (total distance / distance flown).

sub_modules.title: New Track Reference
sub_modules.content: Preferred method using proportional correction based on progress.

sub_modules.title: Practical Map Techniques
sub_modules.content: Using 10° guidelines and distance-to-go markers.

chapter_name: Chapter 12 - Other Applications of the 1 in 60 Rule

sub_modules.title: Height on a Glide Slope
sub_modules.content: Height = Glide slope angle × 100 × distance (NM).

sub_modules.title: Rate of Descent (ROD)
sub_modules.content: ROD = 5 × ground speed (for 3° glide slope). Adjust proportionally for other angles.

sub_modules.title: Change of Speed Effect
sub_modules.content: Change in ROD = 5 × change in speed (for 3° glide slope). Adjust for other glide slopes.

sub_modules.title: VOR/DME Crosstrack Error
sub_modules.content: Distance off = (Track error × distance) / 60.

sub_modules.title: Range from VOR Bearing Change
sub_modules.content: Range = (distance travelled × 60) / change in bearing.

chapter_name: Chapter 13 - Topographical Maps and Map Reading

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: Visual navigation using map, compass, and pilot observation is essential when modern systems fail.

sub_modules.title: Relief Representation
sub_modules.content: Terrain is shown using contours, layer tinting, spot elevations, and MEF/MSA.

sub_modules.title: Maximum Elevation Figure (MEF) and Minimum Safe Altitude (MSA)
sub_modules.content: MEF shows highest obstacle with safety margin; MSA includes additional safety clearance.

sub_modules.title: Map Features
sub_modules.content: Water (blue), woods (green), roads (red), railways (black).

sub_modules.title: Scale
sub_modules.content: Scale can be expressed as words, representative fraction, or graduated scale line.

sub_modules.title: Map Reading Techniques
sub_modules.content: Includes map-to-ground and ground-to-map interpretation.

sub_modules.title: Visual Checkpoints
sub_modules.content: Features used for navigation at regular intervals (5–10 minutes).

sub_modules.title: Selecting Checkpoints
sub_modules.content: Checkpoints should be large, unique, visible, and contrasting.

sub_modules.title: Types of Visual Features
sub_modules.content: Includes coastlines, rivers, mountains, towns, roads, railways, woods, and man-made structures.

sub_modules.title: Visual Navigation Hazards
sub_modules.content: Includes poor contrast, white-out, featureless terrain, and night disorientation.

sub_modules.title: Navigation Discipline
sub_modules.content: Maintain heading, altitude, timing, and situational awareness.

sub_modules.title: Lost Procedure
sub_modules.content: Climb, fix position, use DR, draw uncertainty circle, and navigate to a known feature.

sub_modules.title: Meridians and Convergence
sub_modules.content: Meridians converge towards the poles and are parallel at the equator.

sub_modules.title: Definition of Convergency
sub_modules.content: Convergency is the angle between two meridians at a given latitude.

sub_modules.title: Convergency Formula
sub_modules.content: Convergency = Change in Longitude × sin(mean latitude).

sub_modules.title: Great Circle Track Change
sub_modules.content: Great circle track changes direction due to converging meridians.

sub_modules.title: Mid Latitude Approximation
sub_modules.content: Mid latitude is used instead of mean latitude for simplicity.

sub_modules.title: Conversion Angle
sub_modules.content: Conversion angle is the difference between great circle and rhumb line direction.

sub_modules.title: Conversion Angle Formula
sub_modules.content: Conversion angle = ½ × Change in Longitude × sin(mean latitude).

sub_modules.title: Relationship Between Convergency and Conversion Angle
sub_modules.content: Conversion angle is always half of convergency.

sub_modules.title: Definition of Departure
sub_modules.content: Departure is the east-west distance between two meridians measured along a parallel of latitude.

sub_modules.title: Effect of Latitude
sub_modules.content: Departure is maximum at the equator and zero at the poles due to convergence of meridians.

sub_modules.title: Departure Formula
sub_modules.content: Departure (NM) = Change in Longitude (minutes) × cos(latitude).

sub_modules.title: Basic Departure Calculations
sub_modules.content: Used to find change in longitude or distance when moving east or west.

sub_modules.title: Latitude-Based Departure Conversion
sub_modules.content: Departure varies with cosine of latitude and can be converted between latitudes.

sub_modules.title: Shortcut Formula
sub_modules.content: Departure A / cos A = Departure B / cos B.

sub_modules.title: Problem Solving Techniques
sub_modules.content: Use elimination and logical reasoning for faster answers in exams.

sub_modules.title: Definition of Scale
sub_modules.content: Scale is the relationship between chart distance and actual Earth distance.

sub_modules.title: Statement of Scale
sub_modules.content: Expressed in words such as '1 inch to 10 nautical miles'.

sub_modules.title: Graduated Scale Line
sub_modules.content: Graphical scale shown on charts using distance markings.

sub_modules.title: Representative Fraction (RF)
sub_modules.content: Scale expressed as a ratio: Chart Length / Earth Distance.

sub_modules.title: Conversion to RF
sub_modules.content: Convert both chart length and Earth distance into same units before forming ratio.

sub_modules.title: Scale Calculations
sub_modules.content: Use RF = Chart Length / Earth Distance for solving problems.

sub_modules.title: Unit Conversions
sub_modules.content: Important conversions include NM to meters/feet and inches to cm.

sub_modules.title: Types of Scale Problems
sub_modules.content: Includes finding RF, chart distance, or Earth distance.

sub_modules.title: Large vs Small Scale
sub_modules.content: Large scale = more detail, small area; Small scale = less detail, larger area.

sub_modules.title: Chart Projections
sub_modules.content: Projection is the method of transferring Earth's curved surface onto a flat chart.

sub_modules.title: Perspective vs Non-Perspective
sub_modules.content: Perspective charts are geometric projections; non-perspective charts are mathematically derived.

sub_modules.title: Reduced Earth
sub_modules.content: A scale model of Earth used as the basis for chart projection.

sub_modules.title: Types of Projections
sub_modules.content: Three main types: Azimuthal (Plane), Cylindrical, and Conical projections.

sub_modules.title: Azimuthal Projection
sub_modules.content: Flat plane projection used mainly for polar regions.

sub_modules.title: Cylindrical Projection
sub_modules.content: Projection onto a cylinder, commonly used for Mercator charts.

sub_modules.title: Conical Projection
sub_modules.content: Projection onto a cone, used for mid-latitude charts like Lambert Conformal.

sub_modules.title: Ideal Chart Properties
sub_modules.content: Includes correct angles, scale, shape, straight rhumb/great circles, and global coverage.

sub_modules.title: Limitations of Charts
sub_modules.content: Perfect scale and shape cannot be achieved on flat charts.

sub_modules.title: Orthomorphism (Conformality)
sub_modules.content: Property where angles are preserved, essential for navigation.

sub_modules.title: Conditions for Conformality
sub_modules.content: Meridians and parallels intersect at 90° and scale is equal in all directions at a point.

sub_modules.title: Mercator Projection
sub_modules.content: A cylindrical projection modified mathematically to achieve conformality.

sub_modules.title: Historical Background
sub_modules.content: Developed by Gerardus Mercator in 1569 to correct distortions in simple cylindrical projections.

sub_modules.title: Orthomorphism
sub_modules.content: Mercator charts are conformal, preserving angles for accurate navigation.

sub_modules.title: Scale Variation
sub_modules.content: Scale increases with latitude as secant of latitude and is correct at the equator.

sub_modules.title: Graticule
sub_modules.content: Meridians are parallel and equally spaced; parallels are parallel but spaced increasingly with latitude.

sub_modules.title: Shape Distortion
sub_modules.content: Shapes and areas become distorted at higher latitudes.

sub_modules.title: Chart Convergence
sub_modules.content: Zero everywhere due to parallel meridians.

sub_modules.title: Rhumb Lines
sub_modules.content: Straight lines on Mercator charts representing constant direction.

sub_modules.title: Great Circles
sub_modules.content: Appear as curved lines (except equator and meridians).

sub_modules.title: Conversion Angle
sub_modules.content: Half of Earth convergency; used to convert rhumb line to great circle direction.

sub_modules.title: Practical Navigation Use
sub_modules.content: Useful for constant heading navigation but not shortest distance routes.

sub_modules.title: Mercator Scale Principle
sub_modules.content: Scale increases with latitude as the secant of latitude.

sub_modules.title: Scale Formula
sub_modules.content: Scale at latitude = Scale at Equator × sec(latitude).

sub_modules.title: Types of Scale Problems
sub_modules.content: Includes conversions between equator and latitude, between latitudes, and distance calculations.

sub_modules.title: Scale Expansion
sub_modules.content: Scale expands away from the equator, meaning denominator decreases.

sub_modules.title: Scale Conversion Between Latitudes
sub_modules.content: Uses cosine ratio of latitudes to compare scale denominators.

sub_modules.title: Distance Between Meridians
sub_modules.content: Chart distance between meridians is constant on Mercator charts.

sub_modules.title: Area of Constant Scale
sub_modules.content: Scale is approximately constant within 8° latitude from the equator.

sub_modules.title: Exam Techniques
sub_modules.content: Use logic and elimination based on scale expansion or contraction.

sub_modules.title: Need for Conical Charts
sub_modules.content: Mercator limitations led to development of conical projections for better great circle representation and near-constant scale.

sub_modules.title: Simple Conical Projection
sub_modules.content: Projection using a cone touching Earth at a standard parallel where scale is correct.

sub_modules.title: Standard Parallel
sub_modules.content: Latitude where the cone touches the Earth and scale is correct.

sub_modules.title: Chart Convergence (Simple Conic)
sub_modules.content: Chart convergence = change in longitude × sine of parallel of origin.

sub_modules.title: Lambert Modification
sub_modules.content: Cone is adjusted inside the Earth creating two standard parallels for better scale distribution.

sub_modules.title: Standard Parallels
sub_modules.content: Two latitudes where scale is correct, improving accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Parallel of Origin
sub_modules.content: Midpoint between standard parallels; defines chart convergence.

sub_modules.title: Scale Behavior
sub_modules.content: Scale is correct at standard parallels, contracted between them, expanded outside.

sub_modules.title: Orthomorphism
sub_modules.content: Lambert chart is conformal, preserving angles.

sub_modules.title: Graticule
sub_modules.content: Meridians are straight lines; parallels are arcs centered on the pole.

sub_modules.title: Chart Convergence
sub_modules.content: Constant across chart and depends on sine of parallel of origin.

sub_modules.title: Rhumb Lines
sub_modules.content: Straight only along meridians; otherwise curves concave to pole.

sub_modules.title: Great Circles
sub_modules.content: Nearly straight lines, especially near parallel of origin.

sub_modules.title: Constant Scale Concept
sub_modules.content: Lambert charts are nearly constant scale if distortion is within 1%, allowing ruler measurements.

sub_modules.title: Effect of Standard Parallel Separation
sub_modules.content: Scale error increases as separation between standard parallels increases.

sub_modules.title: Earth vs Chart Convergence
sub_modules.content: Earth convergence varies with latitude, while chart convergence is constant on Lambert charts.

sub_modules.title: Conversion Angle
sub_modules.content: Conversion angle = half earth convergence, used to relate rhumb line and great circle.

sub_modules.title: Chart Convergence Effect
sub_modules.content: Difference between straight line and rhumb line equals half chart convergence.

sub_modules.title: Great Circle Curvature
sub_modules.content: Great circles appear straight at parallel of origin and curve concave towards it elsewhere.

sub_modules.title: Mid-Meridian Property
sub_modules.content: Rhumb line, great circle, and straight line are parallel at mid-meridian.

sub_modules.title: Advantages of Lambert Chart
sub_modules.content: Great circles nearly straight, good for aviation, near constant scale.

sub_modules.title: Disadvantages of Lambert Chart
sub_modules.content: Complex plotting, non-rectangular grid, requires convergence handling.

sub_modules.title: Plotting Bearings
sub_modules.content: Different methods based on whether bearing is measured at ground station or aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Meridian Transfer
sub_modules.content: Used for ADF plotting to account for convergence differences.

sub_modules.title: Introduction
sub_modules.content: A true perspective projection using a plane touching the pole with light source from opposite pole.

sub_modules.title: Graticule
sub_modules.content: Meridians are straight lines radiating from the pole; parallels are concentric circles.

sub_modules.title: Co-Latitude Concept
sub_modules.content: Co-latitude = 90° − latitude, used in scale calculations.

sub_modules.title: Scale Expansion
sub_modules.content: Scale expands away from the pole as sec²(½ co-latitude).

sub_modules.title: Constant Scale Zones
sub_modules.content: Scale within 1% between 90°–78°, and within 3% between 78°–70°.

sub_modules.title: Orthomorphism
sub_modules.content: Chart is conformal; angles are preserved.

sub_modules.title: Shape Distortion
sub_modules.content: Distortion increases away from the pole.

sub_modules.title: Chart Convergence
sub_modules.content: Equal to change in longitude; constant across chart; n = 1.

sub_modules.title: Rhumb Lines
sub_modules.content: Curved lines concave to the pole.

sub_modules.title: Great Circles
sub_modules.content: Curved but less than rhumb lines; nearly straight at high latitudes.

sub_modules.title: Straight Line Approximation
sub_modules.content: At latitudes above 70°, straight lines approximate great circles.

sub_modules.title: Applications
sub_modules.content: Used for polar navigation, long-range flights, and meteorology.

sub_modules.title: Straight-Line Track Problems
sub_modules.content: Solved using geometry and convergence properties.

sub_modules.title: Purpose of Time in Aviation
sub_modules.content: Used to determine standard time zones and daylight/night conditions for flight operations.

sub_modules.title: Solar System Basics
sub_modules.content: Earth rotates on its axis and orbits the Sun, forming basis of time measurement.

sub_modules.title: Kepler’s Laws
sub_modules.content: Planetary motion follows elliptical orbits, equal areas in equal times, and period-distance relationship.

sub_modules.title: Earth’s Orbit
sub_modules.content: Earth orbits the Sun in an ellipse, moving faster at perihelion and slower at aphelion.

sub_modules.title: Seasons
sub_modules.content: Caused by Earth’s axial tilt (23.5°), not distance from Sun.

sub_modules.title: Solstices and Equinoxes
sub_modules.content: Key dates defining seasonal changes and Sun’s position relative to Earth.

sub_modules.title: Declination
sub_modules.content: Angular position of Sun relative to Equator, varies annually between 23.5°N and 23.5°S.

sub_modules.title: Types of Days
sub_modules.content: Sidereal, apparent solar, and mean solar days differ in measurement basis.

sub_modules.title: Sidereal Day
sub_modules.content: Measured against stars; shorter than solar day.

sub_modules.title: Apparent Solar Day
sub_modules.content: Measured using real Sun; varies in length.

sub_modules.title: Mean Solar Day
sub_modules.content: Average solar day; constant and used for civil time.

sub_modules.title: Equation of Time
sub_modules.content: Difference between apparent and mean solar time.

sub_modules.title: Types of Year
sub_modules.content: Sidereal year, tropical year, and calendar year differences.

sub_modules.title: Hour Angle
sub_modules.content: Angular distance of celestial body westward from a reference meridian.

sub_modules.title: Greenwich Hour Angle
sub_modules.content: Hour angle measured from Greenwich meridian, analogous to longitude.

sub_modules.title: Arc to Time Conversion
sub_modules.content: Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours; 15° equals 1 hour and 1° equals 4 minutes.

sub_modules.title: Conversion Methods
sub_modules.content: Arc can be converted using tables or by dividing degrees by 15 and converting decimals.

sub_modules.title: Local Mean Time (LMT)
sub_modules.content: Time based on the position of the mean Sun relative to a local meridian.

sub_modules.title: LMT Properties
sub_modules.content: Sun crossing a meridian gives 1200 LMT; anti-meridian gives midnight.

sub_modules.title: UTC (Coordinated Universal Time)
sub_modules.content: Global time standard equivalent to GMT used in aviation.

sub_modules.title: LMT and UTC Conversion
sub_modules.content: Longitude east → UTC least; longitude west → UTC best.

sub_modules.title: LMT Problem Solving
sub_modules.content: Always convert through UTC to avoid errors, especially with date changes.

sub_modules.title: Zone Time (ZT)
sub_modules.content: World divided into 15° longitude zones, each representing 1 hour difference.

sub_modules.title: Zone Number
sub_modules.content: Longitude divided by 15 gives zone number; east negative, west positive.

sub_modules.title: Zone Time Formula
sub_modules.content: UTC = Zone Time + Zone Number.

sub_modules.title: Standard Time (ST)
sub_modules.content: Country-specific time adjusted from UTC for convenience.

sub_modules.title: Standard Time Lists
sub_modules.content: Countries grouped based on east or west of Greenwich with time corrections.

sub_modules.title: Daylight Saving Time
sub_modules.content: Seasonal adjustment of +1 hour to standard time.

sub_modules.title: International Date Line
sub_modules.content: Date changes when crossing 180° longitude, adjusted for political reasons.

sub_modules.title: Time Conversion Strategy
sub_modules.content: Best approach is LMT → UTC → ST or reverse to avoid mistakes.

sub_modules.title: Sunrise and Sunset Basics
sub_modules.content: Sunrise and sunset occur at same LMT along same latitude; affected by declination and latitude.

sub_modules.title: Effect of Earth’s Tilt
sub_modules.content: Tilt causes seasonal variation in sunrise, sunset, and day length.

sub_modules.title: Equinox Condition
sub_modules.content: Day and night are approximately equal worldwide; sunrise ~0600 and sunset ~1800 LMT.

sub_modules.title: Polar Day and Night
sub_modules.content: Above Arctic/Antarctic circles, continuous daylight or darkness occurs depending on season.

sub_modules.title: Sunrise/Sunset Tables
sub_modules.content: Air Almanac provides LMT values; interpolation may be required.

sub_modules.title: Sunrise/Sunset Calculations
sub_modules.content: Convert LMT values into UTC or Standard Time using arc-time methods.

sub_modules.title: Definition of Night
sub_modules.content: Night defined as period after sunset or between twilight limits depending on authority.

sub_modules.title: Twilight
sub_modules.content: Period of illumination before sunrise and after sunset due to atmospheric refraction.

sub_modules.title: Civil Twilight
sub_modules.content: Sun between 0°50’ and 6° below horizon; sufficient light for normal activities.

sub_modules.title: Nautical Twilight
sub_modules.content: Sun between 6° and 12° below horizon; general outlines visible.

sub_modules.title: Astronomical Twilight
sub_modules.content: Sun between 12° and 18° below horizon; near complete darkness.

sub_modules.title: Twilight Duration
sub_modules.content: Calculated from difference between sunset/sunrise and twilight times.

sub_modules.title: Extreme Twilight Cases
sub_modules.content: At high latitudes, twilight may last all night or Sun may not set/rise.

sub_modules.title: Equatorial Twilight
sub_modules.content: Shortest twilight (~21 minutes) due to vertical Sun path.

sub_modules.title: High Latitude Twilight
sub_modules.content: Twilight duration increases with latitude; may last entire night.

sub_modules.title: Effect of Altitude
sub_modules.content: Higher altitude causes earlier sunrise, later sunset, and shorter twilight duration.

sub_modules.title: Purpose of Grid Navigation
sub_modules.content: Used at high latitudes where meridian convergence becomes large and True North changes significantly.

sub_modules.title: Problem with True Navigation
sub_modules.content: True North direction changes continuously when moving across longitudes.

sub_modules.title: Grid Concept
sub_modules.content: A fixed reference direction (Grid North) is created using a chosen datum meridian.

sub_modules.title: Grid North
sub_modules.content: Direction parallel to the datum meridian used as a constant reference.

sub_modules.title: Grid Track
sub_modules.content: Track measured relative to Grid North; remains constant across longitudes.

sub_modules.title: Convergence
sub_modules.content: Difference between True North and Grid North at a point.

sub_modules.title: Convergence Rules
sub_modules.content: East convergence → True least; West convergence → True best.

sub_modules.title: Grivation
sub_modules.content: Combined effect of convergence and magnetic variation.

sub_modules.title: Grid to Magnetic Conversion
sub_modules.content: Grid ± Grivation = Magnetic direction.

sub_modules.title: Isogrivs
sub_modules.content: Lines joining points of equal grivation.

sub_modules.title: Determining Convergence Direction
sub_modules.content: Depends on hemisphere and position relative to datum meridian.

sub_modules.title: Steering by Compass
sub_modules.content: Uses grivation similar to variation correction in navigation.

sub_modules.title: Steering by Gyro
sub_modules.content: Preferred at high latitudes; avoids magnetic issues.

sub_modules.title: Gyro Errors
sub_modules.content: Includes real drift, earth rate, and transport wander.

sub_modules.title: Transport Wander
sub_modules.content: Effect of meridian convergence on gyro; not corrected in grid navigation.

sub_modules.title: Polar Grids
sub_modules.content: Standard grids used in polar regions with datum meridian (usually Greenwich).

sub_modules.title: Grid/True Conversion
sub_modules.content: Conversion depends on convergence magnitude and direction.

sub_modules.title: Introduction to Plotting
sub_modules.content: Used in intermediate aircraft with radio aids like VOR, DME, ADF, and gyro systems.

sub_modules.title: Charts Used
sub_modules.content: Lambert Conformal and Polar Stereographic charts are primarily used.

sub_modules.title: Plotting Equipment
sub_modules.content: Includes navigation computer, protractor, straight edge, dividers, and pencils.

sub_modules.title: True vs Magnetic
sub_modules.content: Plotting is usually done using True North as reference.

sub_modules.title: Heading
sub_modules.content: Direction aircraft nose points; may be True, Magnetic, or Compass.

sub_modules.title: Fix
sub_modules.content: Aircraft position determined using position lines or navigation aids.

sub_modules.title: Position Line
sub_modules.content: Line along which aircraft is known to be at a given time.

sub_modules.title: Track Made Good (TMG)
sub_modules.content: Straight line between two fixes when one heading is maintained.

sub_modules.title: Ground Speed Calculation
sub_modules.content: Distance over time between fixes gives actual ground speed.

sub_modules.title: Radio Bearings
sub_modules.content: Includes QTE, QDM, QDR and VOR radials.

sub_modules.title: Q Codes
sub_modules.content: QTE: True bearing from station; QDM: Magnetic track to station; QDR: Magnetic bearing from station.

sub_modules.title: VOR Bearings
sub_modules.content: Measured at ground station and given in magnetic.

sub_modules.title: ADF Bearings
sub_modules.content: Measured at aircraft as relative bearing.

sub_modules.title: Relative Bearing Conversion
sub_modules.content: True bearing = True heading + Relative bearing.

sub_modules.title: Reciprocal for Plotting
sub_modules.content: Convert TO bearing into FROM bearing for plotting.

sub_modules.title: DME Fix
sub_modules.content: Distance combined with bearing gives position fix.

sub_modules.title: Two DME Fix
sub_modules.content: Intersection of two arcs gives two possible positions.

sub_modules.title: Cocked Hat
sub_modules.content: Triangle formed by three position lines; center is estimated fix.

sub_modules.title: Climb and Descent
sub_modules.content: Time in vertical equals time in horizontal.

sub_modules.title: Lambert Chart Properties
sub_modules.content: Great circles appear as straight lines.

sub_modules.title: Measuring Tracks
sub_modules.content: Measured using mid-meridian.

sub_modules.title: Plotting Bearings
sub_modules.content: Use nearest meridian or parallel meridian for accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Chart Convergence Effect
sub_modules.content: Affects ADF plotting due to different meridians.

sub_modules.title: ADF Plotting Correction
sub_modules.content: Plot from a line parallel to aircraft meridian.

sub_modules.title: Variation Usage
sub_modules.content: Ground station bearings use station variation; aircraft bearings use aircraft variation.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Compass Basics
sub_modules.content: Uses Earth's magnetic field to indicate direction relative to magnetic north.

sub_modules.title: Variation
sub_modules.content: Angle between true north and magnetic north.

sub_modules.title: Deviation
sub_modules.content: Error caused by aircraft magnetic influences.

sub_modules.title: Purpose of Compass
sub_modules.content: Indicates aircraft heading.

sub_modules.title: Types of Direct Reading Compass
sub_modules.content: Vertical card compass and grid ring compass.

sub_modules.title: Vertical Card Compass
sub_modules.content: Most commonly used; compass card attached to magnet assembly.

sub_modules.title: Grid Ring Compass
sub_modules.content: More accurate but bulky; used in older aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Compass Construction
sub_modules.content: Magnet assembly suspended in liquid with a lubber line for reading.

sub_modules.title: Compass Requirements
sub_modules.content: Must be horizontal, sensitive, and aperiodic.

sub_modules.title: Horizontality
sub_modules.content: Achieved by pendulous suspension to counter vertical magnetic forces.

sub_modules.title: Sensitivity
sub_modules.content: Ability to align with Earth's horizontal magnetic field.

sub_modules.title: Friction Reduction
sub_modules.content: Achieved using jewel pivot, liquid damping, and buoyancy.

sub_modules.title: Aperiodicity
sub_modules.content: Quick settling without oscillation.

sub_modules.title: Damping
sub_modules.content: Provided by liquid and design of magnet assembly.

sub_modules.title: Serviceability Checks
sub_modules.content: Check for damage, fluid clarity, and absence of bubbles.

sub_modules.title: Accuracy Limits
sub_modules.content: Acceptable error is ±10°.

sub_modules.title: Compass Swing
sub_modules.content: Procedure to measure and correct deviation.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Card
sub_modules.content: Displays residual deviation for different headings.

sub_modules.title: Magnetic Interference
sub_modules.content: Ferromagnetic objects and electrical systems affect compass accuracy.

sub_modules.title: Deviation
sub_modules.content: Difference between compass reading and magnetic meridian due to aircraft magnetism.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Naming
sub_modules.content: East if compass points east of magnetic north, West if it points west.

sub_modules.title: Deviation Rule
sub_modules.content: Deviation West → Compass Best; Deviation East → Compass Least.

sub_modules.title: Compass Swing
sub_modules.content: Procedure to measure and correct deviation across headings.

sub_modules.title: Purpose of Compass Swing
sub_modules.content: Determine, reduce, and record deviation.

sub_modules.title: Hard Iron Magnetism
sub_modules.content: Permanent magnetism from aircraft materials; fixed with respect to aircraft.

sub_modules.title: Soft Iron Magnetism
sub_modules.content: Induced magnetism from Earth's field; varies with latitude.

sub_modules.title: Vertical Soft Iron (VSI)
sub_modules.content: Magnetism induced by vertical component of Earth’s field.

sub_modules.title: Coefficient A
sub_modules.content: Mechanical error due to misalignment of compass.

sub_modules.title: Coefficient B
sub_modules.content: Deviation caused by fore-aft magnetism; follows sine curve.

sub_modules.title: Coefficient C
sub_modules.content: Deviation caused by lateral magnetism; follows cosine curve.

sub_modules.title: Correction of Coefficients
sub_modules.content: Adjust compass to minimize A, B, and C errors.

sub_modules.title: Residual Deviation
sub_modules.content: Remaining deviation recorded on deviation card.

sub_modules.title: Accuracy Limits
sub_modules.content: Deviation must be within ±10°.

sub_modules.title: Effect of Latitude
sub_modules.content: Deviation increases towards poles due to weaker horizontal magnetic field.

sub_modules.title: Hard Iron Latitude Effect
sub_modules.content: Deviation increases as horizontal component decreases.

sub_modules.title: Soft Iron Latitude Effect
sub_modules.content: Deviation increases with vertical component (Z).

sub_modules.title: Dip Relationship
sub_modules.content: Deviation varies with tan(dip) = Z/H.

sub_modules.title: Compass Swing Situations
sub_modules.content: Required after maintenance, modifications, shocks, lightning, or relocation.

sub_modules.title: Types of Problems
sub_modules.content: Includes triangle problems, geometry of velocities, rhumb line calculations, and timing problems.

sub_modules.title: Triangular Problems
sub_modules.content: Uses geometry and relative bearings to determine distance and track.

sub_modules.title: Isosceles Triangle Concept
sub_modules.content: Equal angles lead to equal sides; used in CPA problems.

sub_modules.title: Closest Point of Approach (CPA)
sub_modules.content: Minimum distance between aircraft and beacon.

sub_modules.title: Track and Ground Speed Problems
sub_modules.content: Derived using geometry and Pythagoras.

sub_modules.title: Triangle of Velocities
sub_modules.content: Relationship between TAS, wind, and ground speed.

sub_modules.title: Wind Geometry Insight
sub_modules.content: Wind at 90° does not always give zero head/tail component.

sub_modules.title: Mirror Effect
sub_modules.content: Reversing track produces same headwind effect.

sub_modules.title: Rhumb Line Calculation
sub_modules.content: Uses change in latitude and departure to find track.

sub_modules.title: Departure Formula
sub_modules.content: Departure = ΔLong × cos(latitude).

sub_modules.title: Track Angle Calculation
sub_modules.content: tan θ = opposite / adjacent.

sub_modules.title: Timing Problems
sub_modules.content: Based on distance, ground speed, and required arrival time.

sub_modules.title: Ground Speed Calculation
sub_modules.content: Distance divided by time.

sub_modules.title: Mach Number Adjustment
sub_modules.content: Adjusted using TAS and wind component.

sub_modules.title: Wind Component Reversal
sub_modules.content: Wind effect reverses sign when changing direction.
